title: Use regime-gated experts for undercut/overcut and traffic states
summary: Strategy papers and F1 race analysis emphasize that pit timing changes sharply with traffic, tyre warm-up, wet/dry state, and whether an undercut or overcut is viable.
rationale: Strategy papers and F1 race analysis emphasize that pit timing changes sharply with traffic, tyre warm-up, wet/dry state, and whether an undercut or overcut is viable. A single global classifier may blur these regimes. Even without explicit gap telemetry, the dataset contains usable proxies: compound family, same-lap ranks, position, stint age, wet flags, and testing-vs-race context.
implementation_hint: Define a small set of regimes such as dry vs wet, early/mid/late race, post-pit cold-tyre laps, and traffic-sensitive windows using same-lap pace/position proxies. Train separate experts or a gated ensemble per regime, then combine them with OOF logistic stacking. Include features like ‘old tyre + poor same-lap pace rank + trapped position’ as undercut-pressure proxies.
expected_effect: Can capture strategy nonlinearity that a global model smooths away, especially around pit windows where competition context matters most.
risk: Regime slicing reduces sample size and bad gates can add variance; this should be kept to a few interpretable experts, not many micro-models.
